turns.content: I'm working through the embedded exercises in this book and I'm confused about how defmt macros work without std. Isn't it basically just a wrapper around println! like serde_json::Value serialization? Why would we need special macros instead of using standard formatting traits?

That's a common initial assumption, but defmt is fundamentally different because it doesn't rely on the standard library at all. The defmt! macro compiles to a function that writes directly to an RTT transport channel instead of calling fmt::Write or formatting through std::fmt. It generates code that avoids string allocations entirely by encoding values into a fixed-size buffer, which is why you see something like impl DefmtLog for your types rather than standard trait implementations.

Wait, so I don't need to implement Serialize or Clone on my structs? That seems too good to be true. If I just want to log a complex struct, shouldn't I still have to manually derive the traits and handle formatting logic myself like in normal Rust?

You're right that it feels magical at first, but the key is understanding how probe-rs integration handles the actual logging flow. When you use defmt! on a struct, the macro generates code that automatically derives DefmtLog for each field and formats them into the RTT buffer using specialized encoding logic. You don't write any formatting code manually—the compiler does it all based on your type's Defmt trait implementations, so even complex nested structs become trivial to log without manual string manipulation or custom fmt::Display traits.

I'm trying to add logging to my embedded project but I get a compile error saying defmt is not defined. The error message says cannot find type defmt::Event and it seems like the trait isn't implemented on the log macro arguments.

It looks like you are missing the no_std environment setup which requires defining the Defmt logging crate before using it. In a standard library context, you would simply use println! with serde_json or std::fmt::Display traits, but for embedded systems without a full standard library, you must first define the defmt macro and then import the Defmt trait from the defmt crate so that your logs can be serialized to RTT transport.

I added the defmt macro at the top of my file and imported the Defmt trait now I get an error about a missing function in the log wrapper. It seems like the log! macro isn't accepting the right arguments or the formatting is failing because it expects something specific from the RTT transport layer.

The issue here is that you are likely trying to use standard std::fmt traits instead of defmt macros which require a different interface. The Defmt trait requires logging messages using the defmt macro syntax and then calling log! with those strings, but you need to ensure your code is actually targeting no_std mode via cfg attributes so that the compiler knows not to link against std libraries. The probe-rs debugger integration expects these logs in a specific format that matches what RTT transport can handle.

I managed to get it compiling with the right cfg flags but now I'm getting weird output on my terminal that doesn't look like normal text. It seems like the logging is happening but the formatting is producing strange symbols instead of readable strings because the defmt! macro isn't properly handling the string interpolation.

That's actually expected behavior for Defmt without proper configuration. The strange symbols you are seeing are coming from the defmt! macro attempting to format variables that haven't been properly converted to the correct type representation for RTT transport. In a typical no_std environment, you would need to ensure your logging strings are using simple types like string slices or integers rather than complex objects unless those specific types implement Defmt traits.

Ah wait now I understand! The defmt! macro only accepts simple scalar values and basic string literals so when I tried to log a struct directly it failed because that struct doesn't implement the Defmt trait. My aha moment is that for logging complex data in no_std, I need to either use a custom implementation of the Defmt trait or serialize my structs before passing them to the defmt! macro.

Exactly right. You now understand that Defmt requires every type being logged to implement its own traits so that RTT transport can properly decode it. This is why you cannot just pass arbitrary Rust types directly without ensuring they have been configured to work with no_std logging systems.

I'm following up this blog post on embedded logging and I'm frustrated because the defmt! macros seem to just print strings directly without any formatting logic. Why does this even work this way when I need to format arguments manually?

The defmt library is designed specifically for no_std environments where you cannot rely on the standard library's formatting traits like fmt::Display or Write. Instead of using serde_json::Value or std::io::Write, defmt uses a custom format spec that maps to a simple integer representation which is then transmitted over an RTT transport channel. You don't need to implement impl Serialize for your types here because the logging system handles the encoding internally.

Wait, so if I'm not using standard formatting traits, how does my code actually compile and run with these macros? It feels like magic that defmt can just take any type and convert it to log output without me writing a custom formatter for every struct.

That's a great question. Actually, there is a catch here because not all types automatically support defmt logging unless you explicitly define the necessary traits for them. If your struct doesn't derive Defmt or implement the required trait methods manually, it will fail to compile. Do you have a specific type in mind that isn't working right now?
I'm following a talk on embedded Rust and trying to set up defmt logging, but I get this error: 'cannot find value or function named defmt' when compiling my main.rs. What went wrong?

Hey! That usually means the crate isn't linked correctly in your Cargo.toml under profile = "dev". You need to add the line with the defmt feature enabled so it compiles to a single binary instead of multiple targets. Also, make sure you're using the right macro syntax for no_std environments.
Ah okay, I added that but now it's complaining about RTT transport not being found when I try to use defmt! macros in my code. Should I be implementing a trait myself?

Not at all, you don't need to implement anything manually if you're using probe-rs correctly. The RTT transport is handled automatically by the debug build configuration when you enable the feature flag in your Cargo.toml file. Just make sure your chip's debugger supports it and probe-rs is configured to use the correct flash memory region.
Why does the defmt! macro even work here when I have no std library at all? It seems like it should just break because there's no runtime to handle formatting or string operations.

You're right that this feels backwards, but the defmt! macro is actually a compile-time construct wrapped in a nightly feature flag. It doesn't rely on std::fmt at all; instead, it generates a custom serialization trait implementation for your types. The real magic happens when you connect Defmt to an RTT transport via probe-rs, which provides the actual runtime channel for logs to flow out of the chip.

I see the macro expands into traits, but I thought I had to implement a custom formatter myself since there's no println! available. How does the compiler know what format string to use without any runtime support?

The defmt crate provides a default implementation for common types like i16, u8, and String, so you don't need to write custom formatters. When the compiler sees your defmt! call, it matches against these pre-existing traits and generates code that writes directly to the RTT transport buffer. Think of it less like a formatting library and more like a hardware abstraction layer that talks directly to the UART pins via probe-rs integration.
Wait, so if I'm not using std::fmt or any string manipulation at runtime, how does defmt actually serialize complex nested structures without crashing?

That's the key misconception though. The types you pass into defmt! don't get formatted as strings at runtime; they're serialized into a binary format that matches the Defmt encoding spec. The compiler generates code to handle this serialization, and only when probe-rs is configured does it convert that binary stream into human-readable text over RTT.
Okay, so I can just use defmt! on any type without writing custom logic? What if I have a struct with optional fields or enums?

You still need to derive the Defmt trait for those types, but once you do that via the nightly feature flag, defmt! handles all the serialization automatically. The compiler will refuse to compile your code if a type lacks this trait implementation, which is why you'll see errors like 'trait bound requires Defmt' until you add the necessary derives.

I'm trying to build a tool to automate my firmware updates, but I thought I'd just use println! like in Java. Since I'm targeting no_std environments now, does println! even work without the standard library?

That's actually a common misconception; println! relies heavily on std::fmt which isn't available in no_std contexts. Instead you should use defmt logging macros like defmt_info or defmt_warn to output messages through the Defmt trait implementation. These macros compile down to simple function calls that don't need formatting buffers.
Got it, so I can just call defmt_info directly? But how do I actually get those messages out of my embedded hardware?

You need to set up an RTT transport layer since Defmt doesn't have built-in output. The defmt crate provides a no_std implementation that works with RTT, which you can integrate into your tool using the probe-rs integration for debugging. This allows messages to be sent over a serial connection from your IDE or terminal.
I see, so I'll need to configure the probe-rs settings to enable Defmt and link against the defmt crate? And will my Java-style logging patterns still apply?

Yes, you configure probe-rs in your tool's config file to use Defmt as the log target. As for Java patterns, they won't translate directly since Defmt macros don't support format strings or complex formatting like Java's String.format; each macro call is a distinct operation that logs a fixed message structure.
That makes sense. So if I want variable data, do I have to manually construct the string before passing it to defmt_info?

Not quite; Defmt supports variadic arguments through macro specialization so you can pass multiple values directly without manual string construction. For example, defmt_info!("Config loaded: {}, {}", config_name, version) works just like a formatted log message but avoids the overhead of std::fmt entirely.

We're seeing our CI/CD pipeline fail because the build artifacts are too large and slow to upload, so I'm considering switching from println! to defmt macros for embedded logging. Can you explain how this helps with file size?

Switching to defmt macros can significantly reduce the binary footprint compared to standard string formatting because defmt avoids allocating runtime memory for buffers or managing complex heap data structures during compilation. Instead of generating code that builds a String object, defmt generates specialized logic that writes formatted values directly into an RTT transport stream over UART, which means no dynamic allocation occurs and the resulting binary size is much smaller than what you'd get from using println! with serde_json::Value or similar serialization traits.

That sounds efficient, but I'm worried about compatibility. Our current code relies on println! for error handling in panic paths. If we switch to defmt macros, will the panic behavior change and could that break our existing test suites?

The panic behavior itself remains consistent because both println! and defmt macros ultimately invoke the same unwind mechanism when a panic occurs, but the output format changes which might confuse tests expecting string logs. If your tests parse stdout expecting printable text, they will fail because defmt outputs binary-like data unless you route it through an RTT transport like probe-rs integration. You need to ensure your test harness is configured to capture UART output or convert defmt logs into readable strings before comparison.

I see the distinction now, but I still think we should stick with println! for now since our team isn't familiar with defmt macros and the current build times are acceptable. Is there any downside to sticking with standard logging in a no_std environment?

The main downside of sticking with println! in a no_std environment is that it requires manual buffer management or heap allocation for every log message, which increases both memory usage and runtime overhead. In tight resource-constrained systems, these allocations can trigger GC pauses or exhaust limited stack space, whereas defmt macros are designed specifically to avoid this by formatting values into the RTT transport stream without touching the heap at all.

Okay, I understand the performance implications better now. But what about debugging complex state machines where we need to inspect runtime values? Does defmt support that same level of introspection as println!?

Defmt supports full introspection because its macros are designed to format arbitrary Rust types in a way similar to how you'd use serde_json::Value or derive Serialize traits, but with the added benefit of compile-time validation. When you call defmt! on a complex struct, it generates code that correctly handles enum variants and field access without needing runtime reflection, which means your debug output remains clear even for deeply nested state machines.
Great, so we can use defmt macros with no_std environments effectively? But I'm concerned about the learning curve. Are there any crates or tools that make adopting this easier?

The primary tooling ecosystem revolves around probe-rs integration for hardware debuggers and a defmt crate library that provides the core formatting logic. You'll also find useful crates like defmt-rtt which handles the transport layer, but remember to avoid using any multi-threaded synchronization primitives in your logging code since defmt assumes single-threaded execution models.
Thanks for clarifying all this. One last thing - if we do switch, will our existing CI/CD pipeline need major reconfiguration to handle the new binary artifacts?

Yes, you'll likely need to update your artifact handling scripts because defmt logs are written to UART rather than stdout by default in no_std environments. You might want to add probe-rs integration steps or configure your build system to capture serial output specifically for logging analysis.

I'm stuck on a performance issue while reviewing my colleague's embedded code. They're using regular println! for logging in an MCU that has no standard library, and it's causing compile-time errors because std::io::Write isn't available. I noticed they're trying to use the defmt crate which uses the DefmtLog trait instead of Write. Can you explain how defmt macros work without any_std dependencies?

You are correct that println! fails here because it depends on io traits found in std::io. The solution is using the defmt crate which relies on the DefmtLog trait rather than any standard I/O abstraction. When you write a defmt macro like defmt::info!("error occurred"), the compiler generates code that formats strings into a specific format and writes them to the RTT transport layer, avoiding the need for std::io::Write entirely. This allows logging on microcontrollers with minimal RAM and no standard library support.

That sounds efficient but I'm worried about runtime overhead. My colleague's code uses defmt! macros extensively, and I see they're exporting a trait called DefmtLog. Is there any hidden cost to this approach? I want to understand if the formatting logic adds significant latency compared to standard logging.

The primary benefit of defmt is that it avoids runtime string parsing overhead found in standard libraries like serde_json::Value or std::io::Write implementations. The defmt! macro expands at compile time, which means there's no dynamic allocation during execution and the formatting logic is optimized for low-level hardware access. However, you should note that while the defmt trait itself has minimal runtime cost, the RTT transport layer adds some latency because it relies on probe-rs integration to handle the actual data transmission over UART.

I'm concerned about the probe-rs dependency and how it interacts with the DefmtLog trait. If we remove the defmt crate entirely, would we be forced back to using serde_json::Value or similar crates? I want to know if there are truly no_std alternatives that don't require external dependencies.

If you remove the defmt crate and its associated DefmtLog trait, you cannot log effectively without std::io::Write because it requires a standard library implementation. There are no pure no_std crates that provide logging functionality comparable to serde_json::Value or std::io::Write without relying on external hardware drivers like probe-rs. The defmt! macros and DefmtLog trait specifically exist to bridge the gap between high-level Rust syntax and low-level RTT transport in environments where standard libraries are unavailable.

I understand now but I need clarity on how to configure the logging level dynamically at runtime. My colleague's code uses static assertions for defmt macros, and I'm wondering if we can switch from debug to error logging without recompiling? Can we leverage the DefmtLog trait or probe-rs integration to achieve this?

You cannot change logging levels dynamically at runtime because defmt! macros are compile-time constructs that rely on static assertions to determine which log statements are included in the final binary. The DefmtLog trait is used by the compiler during expansion but does not support runtime configuration of verbosity. If you need dynamic filtering, you must implement a custom wrapper around the RTT transport or use alternative logging frameworks like log crate which supports feature flags and environment variables.

That limitation is frustrating for our startup's embedded project where we need flexibility without rebuilding every time. Are there any third-party crates that combine no_std compatibility with dynamic logging control? I'm looking at options similar to serde_json::Value but specifically designed for RTT transport in probe-rs environments.

There are no third-party crates that perfectly replicate the static nature of defmt! macros while adding runtime flexibility without compromising performance. The log crate is the closest alternative as it supports feature flags and environment variables, but it still requires some standard library support or custom implementations for true no_std environments. For your specific use case with RTT transport, you might consider using a simple string buffer before passing data to probe-rs, though this adds memory usage that defmt avoids entirely.
